coredata.dc:title: One-pot synthesis of multicolor carbon quantum dots: One as pH sensor, one with ultra-narrow emission as fluorescent sensor for uric acid
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Danyang
Chao, Daiyong
Yu, Chunyu
Fu, Yao
Zhou, Shihong
Tian, Long
Zhou, Liang
coredata.dc:description: In this work, tricolor carbon quantum dots (CQDs) including blue-, green-, and orange-CQDs were prepared in one-pot. Impressively, the full width at half maximum of blue-CQDs narrowed greatly to 8 nm, serving as the narrowest bandwidth emissive CQDs so far, to our knowledge, which even compared to colloidal semiconductor quantum dots. Based on the blue-CQDs exhibited highly selective and sensitive ‘on-off’ response towards Fe3+ and the outstanding antioxidant properties of uric acid, we constructed ‘on-off-on’ switching sensor to achieve quantitative measurement of uric acid. The green-CQDs served as simple pH sensor qualitatively judging acid rain based on ultrasensitive spectral redshift property at pH < 5.6. The sensors based on CQDs withstood actual sample matrix effects, pH and temperature change, and long-term storage. The ultra-narrow bandwidth emissive CQDs and fluorescent sensors based on distinctive optical properties of CQDs are meaningful and attractive research directions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Narrow full width at half maximum
Multicolor
‘On-off-on’ principle
Uric acid
pH sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: ‘On-off-on’ switching sensor based on b-CQDs to detect UA
Acid rain and soil acidification detection based on g-CQDs
Sensor for UA based on b-CQDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to the financial aid from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 62174160 ) and Research Equipment Development Project of Chinese Academy of Sciences ( YJKYYQ20200005 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of CQDs. TEM images of b-CQDs (a), g-CQDs (b), and o-CQDs (c). Particle sizes distribution histograms of b-CQDs (d), g-CQDs (e), o-CQDs (f) XRD pattern (g), FTIR spectra (h), and the normalized PL spectra (i) of CQDs. Line graphs of element contents (j), high resolution XPS C 1s (k), N 1s (l), and O 1s (m) spectra of CQDs.

Optical characterizations of CQDs. (a–c) UV–vis absorption spectra and PLE spectra of CQDs. (d) Diagram of the PL process of the prepared CQDs. (e) Pseudo-color mapping of the normalized temperature-dependent PL spectra of b-CQDs. (f) Lifetime decay curves of CQDs.

The pH sensor based on g-CQDs. (a) PL spectra of g-CQDs in different pH conditions. (b) PL spectra of g-CQDs in the pH range of 4.4–6.4. (c) Reversibility study of the g-CQDs between pH = 5 and pH = 6.

b-CQDs as fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection. (a) Influence of Fe3+ concentration on the fluorescence intensity of b-CQDs. (b) Linear relationship between F/F 0 of b-CQDs and concentrations of Fe3+. (c) Linear calibration plot for Fe3+ detection in deproteinized fetal bovine serum. (d) The relationship between fluorescence intensity ratios (F/F 0) of b-CQDs and the added volume of deproteinized fetal bovine serum. (e) Absorption spectra of b-CQDs, Fe3+@b-CQDs, and Fe3+. (f) The lifetime delay curves of b-CQDs and Fe3+@b-CQDs.

Determination of UA based on Fe3+@b-CQDs. (a) Influence of uric acid concentration on the fluorescence intensity of Fe3+@b-CQDs. (b) The linear relationship between F/F 0 of the system and concentrations of uric acid. (c) Anti-interference experiment of UA in Fe3+@b-CQDs system.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of trichromatic CQDs, ‘on-off-on’ switching sensor based on b-CQDs and pH sensor based on g-CQDs.
Examples of carbon quantum dots, colloidal semiconductor quantum dots and rare-earth-based complexes with narrow FWHM.

row.entry: Cellular uptake imaging and iron(III) sensing
Phosphor-converted white light emitting diodes and field emission displays

head.ce:title: One-pot synthesis of multicolor carbon quantum dots: One as pH sensor, one with ultra-narrow emission as fluorescent sensor for uric acid

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, tricolor carbon quantum dots (CQDs) including blue-, green-, and orange-CQDs were prepared in one-pot. Impressively, the full width at half maximum of blue-CQDs narrowed greatly to 8 nm, serving as the narrowest bandwidth emissive CQDs so far, to our knowledge, which even compared to colloidal semiconductor quantum dots. Based on the blue-CQDs exhibited highly selective and sensitive ‘on-off’ response towards Fe3+ and the outstanding antioxidant properties of uric acid, we constructed ‘on-off-on’ switching sensor to achieve quantitative measurement of uric acid. The green-CQDs served as simple pH sensor qualitatively judging acid rain based on ultrasensitive spectral redshift property at pH < 5.6. The sensors based on CQDs withstood actual sample matrix effects, pH and temperature change, and long-term storage. The ultra-narrow bandwidth emissive CQDs and fluorescent sensors based on distinctive optical properties of CQDs are meaningful and attractive research directions.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Narrow full width at half maximum

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Narrow emission fluorescent materials (full width at half maxima, FWHM <30 nm) with high color purity are important in optical sensing [1], bioimaging [2], photoelectric devices [3], laser [4], and superresolution [5]. Currently, they mainly include rare-earth-based complexes and colloidal semiconductor quantum dots, which typically involve heavy metal elements and hamper practical applications [6–8]. Similar to colloidal semiconductor quantum dots, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) possess bright photoluminescence (PL) due to quantum confinement effect, which have numerous unique advantages, such as low toxicity, high solubility, ease of modification, ideal chemical stability and high photo-bleaching resistance [9–11]. CQDs possess promising applications in biological imaging, drug loading, fluorescent probe, and phototherapy [12–15]. Narrow-bandwidth emissive CQDs are helpful in effectively improving color-purity, so higher resolution fluorescence signals can be obtained to resist the interference of background signals in the application of fluorescent sensors. However, the FWHM of CQDs is generally greater than 60 nm due to various N, O-containing functional groups including amino, carboxyl, carbonyl, epoxy, and hydroxyl at the edge or basal plane sites [16,17]. In recent years, the development of narrow-bandwidth emissive CQDs is still at the infancy stage, despite sporadic cases of FWHM as narrow as 20–30 nm have appeared [18]. Consequently, designing highly efficient narrow‐bandwidth emissive CQDs and clarifying PL mechanism underpinning the narrow FWHM are of significant importance and urgently needed.

Every coin has two sides, and the N, O-containing functional groups of CQDs are no exception, for one thing, deteriorating color-purity, for another, regulating photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), acting as sensitive antennas to connect with various substances, and convenient for labeling or surface modification [19,20]. Such impressive characteristics have sparked intense interest in using CQDs as fluorescent sensors. Kurniawan et al. constructed pH sensor based on the PL emission of NGQD red-shifted with increasing pH value [21]. Yu and coworkers achieved selective detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ based on significant quenching of emissive C-dots in different buffer solutions [22]. Moreover, relying on the interaction between targets to realize the determination of substances that cannot be detected directly further expands the application range of CQDs as fluorescent sensors. For example, Xu et al. realized the gradual monitor of Fe3+, pyrophosphate anions (PPi) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) by cleverly using the hydrolysis reactions and competition between substances [23]. Inspired by this, we notice that uric acid (UA) cannot directly interact with CQDs but has practical detection needs, for UA content is a sensitive index in the diagnosis of severe renal impairment [24]. Notably, UA is an important reductant and responsible for 50% of the antioxidant capacity of plasma [25]. Besides, Fe3+ has relatively strong oxidizing property and various CQDs were used as fluorescent probes for Fe3+ [26–28]. Therefore, our strategy is to introduce Fe3+, which interacts with both fluorescent CQDs and UA, to detect UA.

Herein, blue-CQDs (b-CQDs), green-CQDs (g-CQDs), and orange-CQDs (o-CQDs) were synthesized by one-pot solvent-thermal method (see Scheme 1 ). The b-CQDs possessed ultra-narrow FWHM of 8 nm, and our study suggested that high content of CC and pyrrole N, as well as the lack of O-containing functional groups, had an effect on narrow FWHM. Then, the ‘on-off-on’ fluorescent switching sensor was assembled for sequential dual-target determination, and the quantitative detection of UA was realized. Regarding g-CQDs, their PL spectra red-shifted 30 nm when pH < 5.6, which acted as pH sensor to determine whether the acidity of rainwater and soil exceeded the standard.
Details of experimental materials, characterization, synthesis and separation of tricolor CQDs, and detection of Fe3+ and uric acid in actual samples are listed in Supplementary material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ‘On-off-on’ switching sensor based on b-CQDs to detect UA
ce:section.ce:para: Detection of Fe3+: A group of FeCl3 solutions (0–1500 μM) with a gradient of concentration were prepared, then, 10 μL b-CQDs (4.5 × 10−5 mg mL−1) were added, and the PL spectra of each sample at 300 nm excitation were recorded. The standard curves of fluorescence quenching were established. Detection of UA Based on Fe3+@b-CQDs: At 37 °C under stirring, 600 μM UA standard stock solution was accurately configured. Varied volumes of standard stock solution were accurately transferred to the quenched Fe3+@b-CQDs solution, and ultra-pure water was added to the total volume of 1.6 mL. The resulting solutions were incubated for 10 min at 37 °C. The fluorescence intensity of each sample at 395 nm was recorded under excitation wavelength of 300 nm. All measurements were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acid rain and soil acidification detection based on g-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The pH = 4.4–6.4 acid solutions were prepared with a pH = 0.2 gradient according to calculated amounts. 1500 μL acid solution, 50 μL ethanol and 50 μL g-CQDs (5.0 × 10−5 mg mL−1) solution were mixed well, and the PL spectra were measured under 360 nm excitation. Qualitatively determine whether the acidities of the water and soil samples exceed the standard: Fresh snow was collected and settled for 2 h until the snow melted completely, then impurities were removed with a 0.22 μm filter membrane. 20 g of air-dried soil was weighed and passed through a 100-mesh aperture sieve into a 50 mL beaker, 40 mL water was added, then stirred for 1 min to fully disperse the soil particles, and filtered after settling for 30 min. Finally, 1500 μL prepared actual sample, 50 μL ethanol and 50 μL g-CQDs solution were mixed well, and the PL spectra were measured under excitation at 360 nm. The 50 μL ethanol was added to enhance the solubility of g-CQDs. All samples were determined in triplicate.

The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of CQDs exhibited both high intensity peaks in the range of 210–280 nm and broad bands in UV–vis regions (300–600 nm). The former originated from the π-π* transitions of unsaturated bonds (CC/CN/CO/NO), and the latter could be ascribed to the n-π* transitions of unsaturated bonds with heteroatoms (CN/CO/NO) (Fig. 2 a–c), indicating the existence of various electronic states in CQDs [46,47]. To clearly understand PL processes, the photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and absorption spectra of CQDs were compared. Only absorption spectra showed obvious peaks in the 210–280 nm range, indicating that the n-π* transitions directly participated in the formation of fluorescence, while the π-π* transitions underwent vibrational relaxation and deactivated back to the ground state. The abundant functional groups form various energy levels, which result in multiple peaks in the PL spectra of CQDs, as shown in Fig. 2d [31,48–50]. Anymore, the relative intensity of triple peaks varied with temperature (Fig. 2e), which attributed to the activity of functional groups changed. The prepared CQDs showed excitation independent property, for their emission peaks did not shift with excitation wavelength changing (Fig. S3). The fluorescence decay curves under emission peaks were detected and fitted with bi-exponential functions (formula S1 and S2). The average lifetimes of tricolor CQDs were 8.17, 4.65, and 3.76 ns, respectively (Fig. 2f).

The obtained CQDs exhibited excellent photo-thermal stability, whose fluorescence intensity remained stable after 70 min UV exposure or 30 min in a water bath at 55 °C (Figs. S4a–f). Then, the salt tolerance and pH stability tests of the prepared CQDs are shown in Fig. 3 a, S4g-i and S5a,b. In high concentration NaCl and acidic solutions, the main peaks of g-CQDs showed special PL spectral redshift characteristics. High concentration of NaCl would promote the ionization of water and produce H+ ions, which is consistent with the acidic solution also containing a certain amount of H+ ions. Once the hydroxyl group on the surface of g-CQDs combines with H+ ion, the PL spectrum would redshift and PL intensity would also be weakened due to the decrease of electron density.

Notably, once the pH value of acidic solution changed from 6 to 5 (Fig. 3a), the PL spectra of protonated g-CQDs would red-shift obviously. Further detailed tests were carried out, as shown in Fig. 3b, the main emission peak of g-CQDs completely recovered to ∼500 nm when pH ≥ 5.6, meeting the detection requirements of acid rain by chance. As we all know, acid rain refers to precipitation with pH below 5.6, which causes irreversible damage to buildings and plants. Moreover, the growth of plants has certain requirements on soil acidity, for example, the suitable pH range for tea tree and tobacco is above 5.5. We conducted pH measurements on soil samples and water samples after snow melted in the garden of CIAC. As shown in Figs. S5c–h, the emission peaks of g-CQDs are ∼500 nm, indicating all samples were qualified, and this conclusion was consistent with that of conventional pH detection methods including pH meter and universal pH indicator paper. Consequently, it is feasible to construct pH sensor based on g-CQDs for qualitative detection of acid precipitation and soil acidification. The ambient pH cycle changing tests between 5 and 6 on fluorescence intensity of g-CQDs showed excellent reproducibility over 5 cycles (Fig. 3c). Therefore, CQDs in sensing applications have incomparable advantages in terms of stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensor for UA based on b-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The high levels of purines ingested by some people lead to gout, a disease that causes great suffering and is affecting younger people. The timely detection of UA levels can help prevent the occurrence of gout, so we hope to make it possible to measure UA with CQDs. However, utilizing CQDs for the direct determination of UA is not easy to realize. Considering that UA and b-CQDs are both electron-rich, the determination of UA can be achieved with the help of Fe3+, an oxidizing ion. When the concentration of b-CQDs was kept constant and Fe3+ was stepwise added, the gradually decreased fluorescence intensities of b-CQDs under excitation of 300 nm were recorded in Fig. 4 a. According to Stern-Volmer theoretical equation,
(1)
F 0/F = 1 + K SV c q
where, K SV is quenching constant, c q is concentration of the quencher (Fe3+), F 0 and F are the maximum PL intensities of b-CQDs without and with Fe3+, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4b, the linear relationships were presented as two-segment, F 0/F = 0.03 x+1.055 (correlation coefficient R2 = 0.877, linear range 3–33.42 μM) and F 0/F = 0.0069 x+1.827 (R2 = 0.99, linear range 33.42–500 μM), with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.963 μM (3δ/S, where δ represents the standard deviation of fluorescence intensity for five blank samples and S represents the slope of the fitting lines.). At low concentrations, the signal detection was more sensitive, indicating that there was a certain force between b-CQDs and Fe3+. When the concentration of Fe3+ attained quite high, the decay of signals slowed down, due to the preferred binding sites on b-CQDs were occupied by Fe3+. The fluorescence intensities of b-CQDs before and after adding Fe3+ in the presence of different metal ions were shown in Fig. S6a. The same degree of fluorescence quenching occurred in each system, suggesting that b-CQDs have good anti-interference ability for detecting Fe3+ in aqueous medium. Furthermore, b-CQDs were used to determine Fe3+ concentration in actual samples to verify the accuracy of the proposed sensor. Firstly, the results of the conducted fluorescent sensor and ICP test for Fe3+ concentration in fertilizer were 4.033 and 4.123 mM, respectively. Secondly, the results of fluorescent sensor with standard addition method (Fig. 4c and d) and ICP test showed the concentration of Fe3+ in deproteinized fetal bovine serum was 6.45 and 6.16 μM, respectively, indicating that b-CQDs have practicability in actual samples for Fe3+ detection. Furthermore, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of b-CQDs by Fe3+ was attributed to dynamic quenching. There was no significant overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Fe3+ and PL spectrum of b-CQDs, which excluded the inner filter effect (Fig. S6b). As shown in Fig. 4e, UV–vis absorption spectrum of b-CQDs did not shift or generate new peaks after adding Fe3+, and the lifetimes of b-CQDs also shortened (Fig. 4f) from 8.17 to 3.09 ns. Therefore, no new complexes were generated but the dynamic collision between Fe3+ and b-CQDs caused the fluorescence quenching. The valence electron arrangement of Fe3+ is 3 d5, and the surface of b-CQDs was rich in electron-donors, so dynamic metal coordination bonds formed [51].

Based on the above inferences, we proposed a strategy to develop an ‘on-off-on’ sensor for quantitating UA. Since UA competed with b-CQDs to reduce Fe3+, it was possible to recover the fluorescence of b-CQDs partly, and also realized the quantitative detection of UA. We investigated the feasibility of the proposed sensor for quantitating UA. As shown in Fig. S7a, only uric acid increased the fluorescence intensity of Fe3+@b-CQDs system among potential interfering compounds in serum samples, which also demonstrates the selectivity of the constructed fluorescent probe. The experimental conditions were also optimized, as shown in Figs. S7b and c. To ensure adequate response and obtain stable fluorescence signals, the incubation temperature and time were determined to be 37 °C and 10 min, respectively. The performance of Fe3+@b-CQDs (1000 μM) towards UA detection was assessed by adding UA at various concentrations from 50 to 550 μM. With increasing UA concentration, as shown in Fig. 5 a, fluorescence intensity of UA/Fe3+@b-CQDs mixture increased steadily. Excellent linear relationship (y = 0.945–0.0007165 x) between F 0/F and UA concentrations (67.64–450 μM) was observed with R2 = 0.995 (Fig. 5b). The LOD was calculated to be 67.64 μM (3δ/S), and the normal range of serum UA in humans is 142–340 μM. The yellow color of solution gradually become shallow, Fe3+ was reduced to Fe2+, and the fluorescence intensity was recovered to 66.45% of original value. The hypothesis that the reason for fluorescence recovery was that UA donated electrons to Fe3+ instead of b-CQDs, was further supported by the experimental results that NaBH4 also caused the fluorescence recovery, as shown in Fig. S8. Several small molecules and ions concomitant with UA in serum were selected for anti-interference experiment against Fe3+@b-CQDs probe, as shown in Fig. 5c, the system still presents virtuous selectivity towards UA (c interfering substance = c uric acid). In addition, as shown in Table S2, the recovery of UA in fetal bovine serum and human urine achieved 101.05%–104.98%. The comparison of UA detection by different research works is depicted in Table S3. The proposed method is sensitive enough for UA detection and is comparable with other methods reported. Although this assay is not optimal in terms of LOD and linear range, whilst the combination of uric acid and CQDs demonstrates an attractive sensor platform for fast, low-cost, high-efficiency, environmental-friendly uric acid detection. This strategy avoids fragile uricase and highly toxic luminescent materials.

In summary, tricolor CQDs were obtained via one-pot solvent-thermal reaction and two of them were developed into applicable sensors in this study. The b-CQDs exhibited a rare ultra-narrow FWHM of 8 nm, which was attributed to high content of CC and pyrrole N, as well as the lack of O-containing functional groups. The b-CQDs showed linear response toward Fe3+, and electron transfer occurred when electron-deficient Fe3+ dynamically combined with electron-rich functional groups of b-CQDs. On the basis of Fe3+@b-CQDs, ‘on-off-on’ switching sensor was constructed and realized the quantitative detection of UA. A considerable amount of literature has been published on CQDs for Fe3+ detection, and we further developed them for the detection of UA. Moreover, the emission peak of g-CQDs exhibited an obvious redshift owing to the functional groups being protonated in acidic solution, so a simple determination method for acid rain and soil acidification was proposed. Importantly, the sensors exhibited strong anti-interference ability, and have been successfully applied to actual samples. This work is committed to exploiting further understanding of narrow-bandwidth emissive CQDs, and exerting the unique advantages of CQDs to facilitate the development of CQDs-based sensors.

Danyang Zhang: Methodology, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Daiyong Chao: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Chunyu Yu: Data curation, Formal analysis. Yao Fu: Software. Shihong Zhou: Resources. Long Tian: Resources. Liang Zhou: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to the financial aid from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 62174160) and Research Equipment Development Project of Chinese Academy of Sciences (YJKYYQ20200005).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article.
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2023.111201.
